skill.name: wp-performance
skill.description: WordPress performance optimization guide for Core Web Vitals, caching, media compression, asset loading, database cleanup, CDN setup, and speed testing.
skill.summary: Optimize WordPress speed with Core Web Vitals guidance, caching, media compression, CDN setup, and performance testing workflows.
skill.author: CrazySwami

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, environment, and blocker patterns, but review shows they are mainly fenced documentation examples for WordPress performance work. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, hidden code, or malicious intent was found. The remaining risk is operational: database cleanup and WP-CLI examples can delete production data if run without backups or staging validation.

medium_findings.title: Destructive WordPress Cleanup Commands
medium_findings.description: The guide includes direct SQL DELETE statements and a WP-CLI deletion command for revisions, expired transients, and orphaned metadata. These are legitimate maintenance examples, but they can permanently remove production data if copied without backups, review, or staging tests.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The destructive commands are explicitly present and target WordPress database records. The intent appears administrative, not malicious, so the issue is operational safety rather than abuse.

medium_findings.title: Broad Tool Permissions for a Documentation Skill
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash, WebFetch, and WebSearch access. These tools match speed testing and command examples, but they increase risk if an assistant executes examples against a live site without user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permissions are visible in the skill header and align with the guide content. The risk depends on runtime behavior because the file itself does not execute commands.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The numerous external command detections occur inside Markdown code fences for PHP, Bash, SQL, Apache, JavaScript, and Python examples. They are not executable skill scripts, but users should treat them as examples requiring review before use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review confirms the patterns are fenced snippets rather than runnable package code. Some examples can still affect local systems if copied directly.

low_findings.title: Network and API Key Patterns Are Example Usage
low_findings.description: The WebPageTest and PageSpeed examples call public performance APIs and use a placeholder API key parameter. No evidence found that the skill reads real secrets or sends credentials to an unexpected endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines show public testing services and an optional api_key variable. There is no environment access or hidden transmission of stored credentials.

low_findings.title: Static Blocker Patterns Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm and reconnaissance detections do not match the reviewed semantic context. The flagged content describes WordPress performance targets, local Redis configuration, Apache cache headers, and public testing tools.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review found no cryptographic implementation, system reconnaissance workflow, or network scanning intent. The evidence fits ordinary WordPress performance documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Deletion Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The WP-CLI revision cleanup example uses command substitution to pass listed revision IDs into a deletion command. This can remove many posts if the selection is broader than expected.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution and deletion action are explicit on the same line. The command is a legitimate maintenance pattern but has destructive effects.

dangerous_patterns.title: Direct SQL Data Deletion Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The database optimization section includes DELETE statements against WordPress posts, options, and postmeta tables. These examples should be run only after backups and table-prefix verification.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The SQL statements are directly visible and destructive. Their administrative purpose is clear, but misuse can cause data loss.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Performance API Calls
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide demonstrates calls to Lighthouse, WebPageTest, and PageSpeed services. These calls are appropriate for speed testing, but they send target URLs to third-party services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Network calls are explicit and target known performance testing services. The risk is disclosure of tested URLs, not malicious exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Slow WordPress sites lose traffic, conversions, and search visibility. This skill gives practical steps for caching, media compression, asset loading, database cleanup, CDN setup, and speed testing.
actual_capabilities: Explains Core Web Vitals targets for LCP, INP, and CLS.
Recommends image and video optimization settings for WordPress sites.
Provides caching guidance for LiteSpeed, browser caching, Redis, and transients.
Shows asset loading patterns for CSS, JavaScript, and fonts.
Documents database cleanup examples using SQL and WP-CLI.
Lists speed testing workflows using Lighthouse, WebPageTest, and PageSpeed APIs.
limitations: It is a guide, not an automated optimizer or monitoring service.
Commands and SQL examples require review before use on production sites.
Recommendations may need adjustment for each theme, plugin stack, and host.
It does not verify actual Core Web Vitals results without external testing tools.

use_cases.title: Improve a Slow Marketing Site
use_cases.description: Audit images, caching, fonts, scripts, and CDN settings before a campaign launch.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Client Performance Report
use_cases.description: Use the checklist and testing sections to organize measurable optimization work.

use_cases.title: Standardize Build Performance Practices
use_cases.description: Apply repeatable media, asset, caching, and database guidance across WordPress projects.

prompt_templates.title: Find Quick Wins
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my WordPress site setup and identify the fastest performance improvements using this skill. Focus on images, caching, scripts, and fonts.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Core Web Vitals Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Core Web Vitals improvement plan for my WordPress site. Include likely LCP, INP, and CLS causes and safe validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Commands
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the database and WP-CLI maintenance steps before I run them. Identify backup needs, staging checks, and commands that could delete data.

prompt_templates.title: Design Ongoing Monitoring
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a recurring WordPress performance monitoring workflow using Lighthouse, PageSpeed Insights, and manual review. Include metrics, cadence, and reporting format.

output_examples.input: My WordPress homepage has a slow LCP image and many blocking scripts.
output_examples.output: A prioritized action plan covering hero image compression, preload checks, deferred scripts, cache settings, and Lighthouse retesting steps.

output_examples.input: I need to clean an old WordPress database safely.
output_examples.output: A review checklist covering backups, staging tests, table prefix verification, revision retention, transient cleanup, and rollback planning.

output_examples.input: Our agency wants a reusable performance checklist for launches.
output_examples.output: A launch checklist covering media formats, caching, CDN settings, font loading, database maintenance, third-party scripts, and final speed tests.

best_practices: Test each optimization on staging before applying it to production.
Measure before and after changes with the same tools and device profile.
Back up files and databases before running cleanup commands or SQL statements.
anti_patterns: Running database deletion examples directly on production without a backup.
Combining or deferring every script without checking theme and plugin behavior.
Optimizing for one Lighthouse score while ignoring real user experience.

faq.question: Does this skill automatically speed up my WordPress site?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, examples, and checklists that an assistant can use during performance work.

faq.question: Can it help with Core Web Vitals?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains targets for LCP, INP, and CLS and suggests common WordPress optimization steps.

faq.question: Are the database commands safe to run?
faq.answer: They can delete data. Review them carefully and use backups, staging tests, and table-prefix checks.

faq.question: Does it support image and video optimization?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers WebP, image sizing, lazy loading, video compression, poster images, and external hosting options.

faq.question: Which testing tools does it reference?
faq.answer: It references Lighthouse, PageSpeed Insights, GTmetrix, WebPageTest, Chrome DevTools, and related API workflows.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
